surface.name: Use the Clerk CLI to scaffold Next.js and create the first signed-in user
surface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Use the Clerk CLI to scaffold Next.js and create the first signed-in user. The platform router has no universal SDK default, but Clerk's current official Next.js AI prompt defines a CLI-first sequence. This candidate selects its fresh empty-directory npm branch, authenticated Clerk account path, visible prebuilt controls, doctor check, and first-user profile-icon terminal. Prior surface before policy commit: Clerk SDK, application stack, and integration model not selected.

alternatives_considered.name: Next.js App Router quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This complete candidate is explicitly default only after Next.js and its App Router have been selected.

alternatives_considered.name: Account Portal
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Clerk calls this the default implementation model, but it is a hosted user-management interface and does not define the same SDK integration result as the stack quickstarts.

alternatives_considered.name: Clerk CLI automatic framework integration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The CLI can auto-detect supported frameworks and use temporary keys, but the docs note incomplete scaffolding support for some SDKs and still depend on the target repository.

entry_point.developer_intent: Reach a first Clerk authentication result without a selected application stack, SDK, hosted or embedded UI model, or authentication method.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived after discovery.
The application stack, existing versus new project, Account Portal versus embedded components versus custom flow, and authentication identifiers are not selected.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: The Clerk Next.js quickstart ends when the running app shows the signed-in user's profile icon in place of the Sign in and Sign up controls.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The profile icon is visible in place of Sign in and Sign up.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The profile icon is visible in place of Sign in and Sign up.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The profile icon is visible in place of Sign in and Sign up.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: Select application stack, SDK, new or existing project, and Account Portal, prebuilt component, or custom-flow integration model.
The Dashboard route requires a Clerk account and application; CLI temporary development keys are a documented alternative for supported project types.
Choose identifiers and social providers for the development application's sign-up and sign-in flow.
The selected SDK guide determines runtime, package manager, framework project, browser, emulator, device, or extension tooling.

primary_path.action: Choose the application stack and Clerk implementation model, then select its official quickstart or Account Portal guide.
details: The Quickstarts router is grouped into full-stack, frontend, and backend SDKs and includes native mobile and browser extension routes.
Core concepts calls Account Portal the default, prebuilt components recommended for most use cases, and custom API flows the higher-control advanced route.
primary_path.input: Existing application stack, runtime target, UI control needs, repository state, and authentication requirements.
primary_path.success_signal: No shared code or authentication page sequence exists at this router.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The path cannot be atomic until SDK and implementation model are selected.

primary_path.action: Follow the selected route through account or temporary-key setup, application and authentication configuration, SDK installation, credentials, framework integration, runtime launch, and first-user verification.
details: The complete authenticated CLI-first Next.js App Router candidate is preserved below.
Other SDKs have different project scaffolds, environment variables, native or browser permissions, middleware, components, and test interfaces.
primary_path.input: Selected Clerk route, application project, authentication choices, and required local toolchain.
primary_path.output: Route-specific Clerk authentication UI or API result and, where documented, a first authenticated user.
primary_path.success_signal: The selected guide's documented sign-up, sign-in, session, or SDK result is visible in its runtime and Clerk development application.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Account creation, unclaimed temporary keys, invalid credentials, framework version, middleware placement, callback URL, identity verification, provider configuration, native API, build, or browser policy can block different routes.

primary_path.action: Open an empty writable project directory and select npm as the package manager for the fresh Next.js route.
details: The current Next.js CLI prompt says an empty directory must choose a package manager; npm is the default when there is no preference.
primary_path.input: Empty directory and local npm environment.
primary_path.success_signal: The shell is in the intended empty directory and npm is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A nonempty directory follows the existing-project `clerk init` branch; a leftover lockfile or package-manager configuration changes the package-manager choice.

primary_path.action: Run `command -v clerk && clerk --version || echo "Clerk CLI not installed"`.
primary_path.output: Installed Clerk CLI path and version, or the documented not-installed message.
primary_path.success_signal: The CLI state is known before setup continues.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A shell that cannot run the check must be corrected before the install or update decision.

primary_path.action: If the CLI is missing, run `npm install -g clerk`; if it is already available, run `clerk update --yes`.
details: pnpm, yarn, bun, Homebrew, and curl are official install alternatives, but this candidate selects npm.
primary_path.input: The CLI state from the preceding check.
primary_path.success_signal: `clerk` is available at the current version.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Package download, permissions, network, or updater errors must be corrected before authentication.

primary_path.action: Run `clerk auth login` from the project directory.
details: The current Next.js CLI prompt requires this immediately after install or update and before app listing or initialization.
primary_path.input: Current Clerk CLI and browser-capable environment.
primary_path.success_signal: The CLI opens or provides the browser login flow.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Browser launch, callback, network, or CLI session errors block the authenticated route.

primary_path.action: Complete the Clerk login flow in the browser and return control to the CLI.
details: If the developer lacks a Clerk identity, account creation is an access gate, but the CLI-first prompt does not enumerate its current form fields.
primary_path.input: Clerk identity and the browser authentication flow.
primary_path.success_signal: The CLI confirms authentication and can proceed to initialization.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Uncompleted account creation, login, browser callback, or identity recovery leaves the CLI unauthenticated.

primary_path.action: Run `clerk init --framework next --pm npm` in the empty directory.
details: Do not add `--app` by default and do not list applications before init. Only use `--app <application_id>` when the developer already supplied an ID or explicitly chose an existing application.
primary_path.input: Empty directory, npm, and authenticated Clerk CLI session.
primary_path.success_signal: The CLI begins scaffolding the fresh Next.js project and Clerk integration.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Project-generation, framework, package-manager, application-linking, network, or filesystem errors can stop initialization.

primary_path.action: Let `clerk init` scaffold Next.js, install `@clerk/nextjs`, link the project to a Clerk application, pull environment variables, and apply the Next.js provider, middleware, and authentication setup.
details: If init explicitly asks for an existing application but no ID was supplied, run `clerk apps list --json`, show the names and IDs, and let the developer choose.
For Next.js 16 or later the middleware file is `proxy.ts`; for Next.js 15 or earlier it is `middleware.ts`.
primary_path.input: Authenticated init session and any prompt responses requested by the CLI.
primary_path.success_signal: Initialization completes and reports that Clerk is set up in the project.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If init errors or does not finish, the documented fallback is `npm install @clerk/nextjs`, a correctly named file exporting `clerkMiddleware()`, and `ClerkProvider` using the full Next.js quickstart.

primary_path.action: Ensure the layout visibly renders `SignInButton` and `SignUpButton` inside `Show when="signed-out"`, and `UserButton` inside `Show when="signed-in"`, all within `ClerkProvider` inside the body.
details: Reuse generated controls when they are already present instead of duplicating them.
primary_path.output: Visible signed-out and signed-in authentication controls.
primary_path.success_signal: The project contains the provider and the three state-appropriate controls without duplicate UI.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Wrong imports, provider placement, missing controls, or exposed secret configuration must be corrected before runtime testing.

primary_path.success_signal: Doctor reports no unresolved integration issue.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Fix every issue reported by the CLI, then rerun doctor before starting the app.

primary_path.action: Run `npm run dev` from the generated Next.js project.
primary_path.success_signal: The app starts without build, environment, or runtime errors.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Compile, dependency, environment, port, or runtime errors must be corrected and the server restarted.

primary_path.action: Open `http://localhost:3000` and confirm that the signed-out sign-in and sign-up controls are visible.
primary_path.input: Running local Next.js app without a user session.
primary_path.success_signal: Sign in and Sign up are visible before authentication.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing controls indicate an init, environment, provider, component, middleware, or runtime problem that must be fixed before signup.

primary_path.action: Select Sign up and complete the Clerk-hosted authentication flow to create the first test user.
details: The exact identifier, verification, or social-provider screens depend on the Clerk development application's configuration and are not fixed by the CLI-first prompt.
primary_path.input: Test-user identity and access to the configured verification method.
primary_path.output: First Clerk user and active application session.
primary_path.success_signal: Signup succeeds and the browser returns to the app in the signed-in state.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Identity verification, provider, callback, browser, session, or application-configuration failure blocks first-user creation; no universal wait or recovery transcript is documented.

primary_path.action: Verify that the signed-out controls are replaced by the profile icon rendered by `UserButton`.
details: The current CLI-first prompt names successful signup plus the visible profile icon as its completion signal.
primary_path.input: Completed signup and active Clerk session.
primary_path.output: Verified first signed-in Clerk user in the Next.js application.
primary_path.success_signal: The profile icon is visible in place of Sign in and Sign up.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Signup without the signed-in profile icon does not establish the documented terminal; inspect the session, keys, provider, middleware, callback, and browser state.

branches.condition: Next.js App Router with embedded prebuilt components is selected.
branches.path: Create or enter the Next.js app using the documented npm, pnpm, yarn, or bun command; install `@clerk/nextjs`; create or sign into Clerk and create the development application, choosing its identifiers and social providers; copy the Publishable Key and Secret Key from Dashboard Quick Copy into `.env`; create `proxy.ts` in the root or `/src` location and export `clerkMiddleware()` with the supplied matcher, using `middleware.ts` for Next.js 15 or earlier; wrap the app layout with `ClerkProvider` and add signed-out SignIn and SignUp controls plus the signed-in UserButton; run the selected package manager's development command; open `http://localhost:3000`; select Sign up and complete the configured authentication flow; verify that the first user exists and the UI shows the signed-in UserButton.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The local Next.js application completes a real Clerk development sign-up and creates its first user.

branches.condition: Another web, mobile, extension, or backend SDK quickstart is selected.
branches.path: Use that guide's project generator or existing project, package manager and SDK, publishable or secret key placement, provider or middleware, hosted or embedded components, callback and route configuration, runtime launch, and documented sign-up, sign-in, or API verification. Native mobile and extensions can add Native API, development-build, OAuth, extension ID, manifest, permission, and emulator or device steps.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The first authenticated result remains stack-specific and may not use the Next.js URL or components.

branches.condition: The default hosted Account Portal is selected without embedding SDK UI.
branches.path: Create the Clerk account and application, select identifiers and social providers, use the application's hosted Account Portal sign-up or sign-in pages, complete the selected identity verification, and confirm the user and session in the hosted interface and Dashboard.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The first user can be created without application SDK integration, so this is a different boundary from a working in-app component.

branches.condition: The general Clerk CLI no-account temporary-development-key route is selected instead of the authenticated Next.js CLI prompt.
branches.path: Run `clerk init` without a Clerk account in a supported project, or use `clerk init --starter` to bootstrap from a starter template; let the CLI generate temporary development keys, detect the framework, install the appropriate SDK, and scaffold authentication; start the app with the generated instructions; complete first-user signup; if Configure your application appears, select it to claim the generated keys and associate the application with a Clerk account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defers the account and browser-login gate until key claiming, but creates an unclaimed temporary development instance rather than the authenticated account-linked candidate.

friction_gates.description: Application stack, SDK, implementation model, project state, and authentication identifiers must be selected.
Dashboard setup creates a Clerk account and development application; temporary CLI keys can defer claiming for supported projects.
Email, phone, username, social providers, middleware, routes, provider components, and key placement depend on the chosen route.
SDK routes use publishable and sometimes secret keys; key names and exposure rules differ between frontend and backend contexts.
Each quickstart adds a stack-specific package, toolchain, and application scaffold or existing-project requirements.
Package installation, build or dev-server startup, and email, phone, or social-provider authentication can introduce waits.
The first-user candidate requires completing the configured authentication flow and observing the signed-in state, not only rendering Sign up.

sources.title: Set up your Clerk account
evidence_supported: Routing to one of multiple quickstarts

sources.title: AI prompts, Next.js App Router
sections_used: Step 1: Install or update the Clerk CLI
Step 4: Fall back to manual setup when init is incomplete
Step 5: Ensure clear auth controls are visible
evidence_supported: Exact CLI check, update, install, login, and init sequence

uncertainties.question: Which Clerk SDK, application stack, and Account Portal, prebuilt component, custom API, or CLI route should define first success?
uncertainties.impact: The choice changes account timing, project scaffold, dependencies, credentials, files, runtime, authentication pages, and verification.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official quickstart router presents multiple stacks and the core concepts page presents multiple implementation models without a universal platform route.

uncertainties.question: Which verification pages and waits will the first user's chosen identity method require?
uncertainties.impact: Email, phone, username, social OAuth, and multifactor configuration add different screens, callbacks, and waits.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The account setup guide requires identifier and provider selection but cannot prescribe one universal authentication transcript.

uncertainties.question: Should CLI onboarding follow the authenticated Next.js prompt or the general CLI's no-account temporary-development-key route?
uncertainties.impact: The choice moves the Clerk account and browser-login gate before initialization or defers it until temporary keys are claimed.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current Next.js CLI-first prompt requires `clerk auth login` before `clerk init`, while the general CLI guide separately permits `clerk init` without an account using temporary development keys.

excluded_after_success: Production domains, key rotation, security hardening, and deployment
